---
title: "Ch 9 ‚Äî Areas"
subtitle: "Why a parallelogram has base √ó height ‚Äî and Heron's formula in one elegant move"
lesson-id: "kg1.areas"
tier: "kg1"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book I (Planimetry)"
kiselev-chapter: 9
kiselev-chapter-title: "Areas"
kiselev-sections: "Planimetry ¬ß¬ß216‚Äì245 (rectangle, parallelogram, triangle, trapezoid, regular n-gon, circle)"
prerequisites: [kg1.similarity, kg1.regular-polygons]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research, ai-ml]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Are similar-figure ratios second-nature for you? *(Linear $k$ ‚áí
   area $k^2$.)* See [`kg1.similarity`](../07-similarity/index.qmd).
2. Can you state the limit definition of $\pi$ from
   [`kg1.regular-polygons`](../08-regular-polygons/index.qmd)?
3. Can you state and apply the *Pythagorean theorem*?
4. Are you comfortable with the formula
   $\text{rectangle area} = \text{base} \times \text{height}$ as a
   working axiom?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A rectangle. Drag its corners; watch the area update as
$\text{base} \times \text{height}$. Now slide the top edge sideways
(keeping the height the same) ‚Äî you get a parallelogram. **Same
area.**

```{ojs}
viewof base = Inputs.range([1, 6], {value: 4, step: 0.1, label: "Base b"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof height = Inputs.range([1, 5], {value: 3, step: 0.1, label: "Height h"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof shear = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1.5, step: 0.1, label: "Top-edge shift (parallelogram tilt)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 280, padX = 40, padY = 40;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 10]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 6]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  const A = [0, 0], B = [base, 0];
  const D = [shear, height], C = [base + shear, height];

  // parallelogram
  svg.append("polygon")
    .attr("points", `${xS(A[0])},${yS(A[1])} ${xS(B[0])},${yS(B[1])} ${xS(C[0])},${yS(C[1])} ${xS(D[0])},${yS(D[1])}`)
    .attr("fill", "#26a").attr("fill-opacity", 0.20)
    .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);

  // ghost rectangle (base √ó height, same base) for comparison
  svg.append("polygon")
    .attr("points", `${xS(0)},${yS(0)} ${xS(base)},${yS(0)} ${xS(base)},${yS(height)} ${xS(0)},${yS(height)}`)
    .attr("fill", "none")
    .attr("stroke", "#a26").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);

  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
    .attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`Parallelogram area = base √ó height = ${base.toFixed(2)} √ó ${height.toFixed(2)} = ${(base*height).toFixed(2)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY + 6)
    .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#a26")
    .text(`Same as the dashed rectangle ‚Äî sliding the top doesn't change the area.`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

The slide along the top is a *shear*. Shears preserve area ‚Äî that's
the whole secret of why parallelograms have area $= bh$.

## Wonder

1. The rectangle has area $b \cdot h$ ‚Äî we accept that as a working
   axiom (length-times-length). What about the *parallelogram*?
2. A triangle is *half* a parallelogram on the same base and
   height. Does that give a triangle-area formula in a single line?
3. A trapezoid has two parallel bases of length $a$ and $b$, with
   distance $h$ between them. **What is its area?** *(Guess from
   the midsegment.)*
4. The area of a circle is $\pi r^2$. Where does the *square* come
   from?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Parallelogram area $= b \cdot h$ (same base and same height as
the rectangle ‚Äî *shear-invariant*). (2) Triangle area $=
\tfrac{1}{2} b h$. (3) Trapezoid area $= \tfrac{a + b}{2} \cdot h$
‚Äî the midsegment $\times$ height. (4) Area scales by $k^2$ under
similarity (Ch 7); doubling the radius quadruples the area; one
factor of $r$ comes from the circumference, the other from the
*radial dimension*.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 ‚Äî Parallelogram has area $b h$

In parallelogram $ABCD$ (with $|AB| = b$ being the *base* and the
perpendicular distance from $CD$ to line $AB$ being $h$, the
*height*), show that the area is $b \cdot h$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Cut a right triangle off one end and translate it to the other end
to form a rectangle of base $b$ and height $h$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Drop perpendicular $DE$ from $D$ to line $AB$, foot $E$ on segment
$AB$. The right triangle $\triangle ADE$ ‚Äî by the
*translation by $\vec{AB}$* ‚Äî coincides with the right triangle
formed at the other end of the parallelogram. Cutting off
$\triangle ADE$ from one end and gluing it to the other turns the
parallelogram into a rectangle of base $|EB| + |AE| = b$ and height
$h$. Area $= b \cdot h$. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß220 ‚Äî the *cut-and-
slide* proof.)*

(*Edge case:* if the perpendicular foot $E$ lies outside segment
$AB$, a sign-bookkeeping argument with a different cut works. The
formula is unchanged.)
</details>

### Problem 2 ‚Äî Triangle has area $\tfrac{1}{2} b h$

In $\triangle ABC$ with base $|BC| = b$ and altitude $h$ from $A$
to $BC$, show that the triangle area is $\tfrac{1}{2} b h$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Reflect $\triangle ABC$ across the midpoint of one of its sides to
form a parallelogram of which the original triangle is half.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Reflect $\triangle ABC$ across the midpoint $M$ of side $BC$ ‚Äî the
reflected triangle $\triangle A'CB$ together with the original
forms a parallelogram $ABA'C$ (since $A$ and $A'$ are equidistant
from $M$, and the diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other).
This parallelogram has base $b$ and height $h$, hence area $bh$.
The triangle is half. **Triangle area $= \tfrac{1}{2} b h$. QED.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß222.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 ‚Äî Trapezoid area

A trapezoid has parallel bases of lengths $a$ and $b$, with
perpendicular distance $h$ between them. Show its area is
$\dfrac{a + b}{2} \cdot h$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Cut along a diagonal ‚Äî two triangles, both with height $h$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cut along diagonal $BD$. The two triangles $\triangle ABD$ and
$\triangle BCD$ have:
- $\triangle ABD$ base $a$ (along $AB$), altitude $h$, area
  $\tfrac{1}{2} a h$.
- $\triangle BCD$ base $b$ (along $CD$), altitude $h$, area
  $\tfrac{1}{2} b h$.
Sum: $\tfrac{1}{2}(a + b) h = \dfrac{a + b}{2} \cdot h$. **QED.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß223 ‚Äî *the trapezoid is the average of its bases
times the height*.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 ‚Äî Heron's formula

Show that the area of a triangle with sides $a, b, c$ is
$$
A = \sqrt{s(s - a)(s - b)(s - c)},
\quad s = \frac{a + b + c}{2}.
$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Express $h$ (altitude on side $c$) using Pythagoras after splitting
$c$ into two pieces by the foot of the altitude. Then use $A =
\tfrac{1}{2} c h$ and simplify.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Drop altitude $h$ from $C$ to $AB$ (length $c$); let the foot $H$
split $AB$ into $|AH| = m$ and $|HB| = c - m$. Pythagoras: $h^2 =
b^2 - m^2 = a^2 - (c - m)^2$. Solve for $m$: $m = (b^2 - a^2 + c^2)
/ (2c)$. Then $h^2 = b^2 - m^2$. Algebraic simplification (the
*difference of two squares* twice over):
$h^2 = \dfrac{(2bc)^2 - (b^2 + c^2 - a^2)^2}{(2c)^2}$.
The numerator factors:
$(2bc - (b^2 + c^2 - a^2))(2bc + (b^2 + c^2 - a^2))
= (a^2 - (b - c)^2)((b + c)^2 - a^2)
= (a - b + c)(a + b - c)(b + c - a)(a + b + c)
= 16 s (s - a)(s - b)(s - c)$
(after using $s = (a+b+c)/2$).
So $h = \dfrac{2\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}}{c}$. Then
$A = \tfrac{1}{2} c h = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$. **QED.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß234 ‚Äî Kiselev follows Heron's original derivation.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 ‚Äî Area of a regular $n$-gon

Show that a regular $n$-gon with circumradius $R$ has area
$$A_n = \tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin\left(\tfrac{2\pi}{n}\right).$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The polygon decomposes into $n$ congruent isoceles triangles, each
with two sides $R$ and included angle $2\pi / n$ at the centre.
Each triangle area: $\tfrac{1}{2} R^2 \sin(2\pi/n)$. Total:
$\tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin(2\pi/n)$. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß238;
also stated as the result of S1 in Ch 8.)*
</details>

### Problem 6 ‚Äî Area of a circle

Show that the area of a circle of radius $R$ is $\pi R^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Take the limit of the regular $n$-gon area as $n \to \infty$.
$\sin(2\pi/n) / (2\pi/n) \to 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By Problem 5, $A_n = \tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin(2\pi/n) = \pi R^2 \cdot
\dfrac{\sin(2\pi/n)}{2\pi/n} \to \pi R^2 \cdot 1 = \pi R^2$ as
$n \to \infty$. The inscribed regular $n$-gon area is sandwiched
between the inscribed and circumscribed polygon areas (sandwich
argument from Ch 8 Pr 7), and both bracket $\pi R^2$. **Circle area
$= \pi R^2$.** *(Planimetry ¬ß241 ‚Äî Eudoxus' method of exhaustion in
the school-friendly Kiselev form.)*
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Heron of Alexandria** (1st century CE) gave the formula in Problem 4
in his *Metrica* ‚Äî a pragmatic surveyor's manual that also describes
a steam-powered toy and a coin-operated holy-water dispenser. The
formula's striking feature: it computes area from the *sides alone*,
without ever measuring an angle or an altitude. Greek surveyors using
nothing but knotted rope could find a triangle's [area](/glossary.qmd#area)
to high precision. Heron's derivation is the one Kiselev follows in
Problem 4 ‚Äî *difference of two squares, twice over*.
:::

### Problem 7 ‚Äî Areas of similar figures

Two similar figures (any shape) with linear ratio $k$ have areas in
ratio $k^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
We proved this for triangles in Ch 7 Problem 5. For *any* polygon,
triangulate; each triangle has area scaling by $k^2$, so the sum
does. For *any* region (curved boundary), approximate by a sequence
of polygons; the ratio is $k^2$ for each, hence in the limit too.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß242 ‚Äî generalisation of the triangle case to
all plane figures.)*
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Every [area](/glossary.qmd#area) formula in the plane reduces to
**rectangle = base √ó height**. Slide it to a parallelogram (shears
preserve area). Cut it in half for a triangle. Average the bases for
a trapezoid. Triangulate any polygon. Take the regular-$n$-gon limit
for the circle. One axiom, one cascade ‚Äî Kiselev's whole ¬ß¬ß216‚Äì245
in a single sentence.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Area formulas.**

| Figure | Formula |
|--------|---------|
| Rectangle, base $b$, height $h$ | $b h$ *(working axiom)* |
| Parallelogram, base $b$, height $h$ | $b h$ |
| Triangle, base $b$, altitude $h$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} b h$ |
| Trapezoid, parallel bases $a, b$, height $h$ | $\tfrac{a + b}{2} h$ |
| Triangle, sides $a, b, c$ (Heron) | $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$, $s = \tfrac{a+b+c}{2}$ |
| Triangle, two sides $a, b$, included angle $C$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} a b \sin C$ |
| Triangle, inradius $r$, semi-perimeter $s$ | $r s$ |
| Regular $n$-gon, circumradius $R$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} n R^2 \sin(2\pi/n)$ |
| Regular $n$-gon, side $s_n$, apothem $\rho$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} (n s_n) \rho$ = $\tfrac{1}{2}$ perim ¬∑ apothem |
| Circle, radius $R$ | $\pi R^2$ |
| Sector of angle $\theta$ (radians) in circle $R$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} R^2 \theta$ |

**Theorem (similarity scaling).** Similar plane figures with linear
ratio $k$ have areas in ratio $k^2$.

:::

A verse:

> *Rectangle base times height ‚Äî that is the seed.*
> *Slide to a parallelogram, area unchanged ‚Äî that's all you need.*
> *Half a parallelogram is a triangle ‚Äî half base times height.*
> *Average the bases times height ‚Äî the trapezoid is light.*
> *And in the limit of n-gons, the circle's area becomes œÄ r squared.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A trapezoid has parallel bases $5$ and $11$, and the perpendicular
> distance between them is $4$. Find its area.

$A = \dfrac{5 + 11}{2} \cdot 4 = 8 \cdot 4 = 32$. **Area $= 32$.**

### Practice ‚Äî Warm

**W1.** A triangle has base $8$ and altitude $5$. Find the area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 8 \cdot 5 = 20$.
</details>

**W2.** A circle has radius $7$. Find the area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi \cdot 49 = 49\pi \approx 153.94$.
</details>

**W3.** A rhombus has diagonals $6$ and $8$. Find the area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular and bisect each other.
The rhombus is *four* right triangles each with legs $3$ and $4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-diagonals $3$ and $4$. Each of the four triangles has area
$\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 6$. Total $= 24$. *Or:* rhombus
area = $\tfrac{1}{2} d_1 d_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 8 = 24$.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Working

**P1.** A triangle has sides $13, 14, 15$. Find its area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Heron's formula. $s = 21$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 21$. $A = \sqrt{21 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6} = \sqrt{7056} =
84$. *(A famous integer-area triangle.)*
</details>

**P2.** A circle is inscribed in a square of side $10$. A second
circle is circumscribed about the same square. Find the *difference*
between the two circles' areas.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Inscribed circle: radius $5$, area $25\pi$. Circumscribed:
radius $5\sqrt{2}$, area $50\pi$. Difference: $25\pi$.
</details>

**P3.** A regular hexagon has side length $6$. Find its area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
A regular hexagon decomposes into 6 equilateral triangles of side
$s$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each equilateral triangle of side $6$ has area $\dfrac{\sqrt 3}{4}
\cdot 36 = 9\sqrt 3$. Total: $6 \cdot 9\sqrt 3 = 54\sqrt 3 \approx
93.53$.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Stretch

**S1.** Show that for any triangle, $A = rs$ where $r$ is the
inradius and $s$ the semi-perimeter ‚Äî *and* derive Heron's formula
by combining $A = rs$ with $A = \tfrac{1}{2} a b \sin C$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = rs$ proved as Ch 7 K3. Combining with Heron requires the
trigonometric identity $\sin C = 2 \sin(C/2) \cos(C/2)$ and
half-angle formulas in the triangle (using the law of cosines).
The two identities yield the same expression; the unification is
**$A^2 = s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)$**. *(See Planimetry ¬ß245 for Kiselev's
derivation.)*
</details>

**S2.** A circle of radius $r$ is inscribed in a sector of angle
$\theta$ in a larger circle of radius $R$. Show that
$r = \dfrac{R \sin(\theta/2)}{1 + \sin(\theta/2)}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
The inscribed circle is tangent to both radii of the sector and to
the arc. Set up an equation using the distance from the centre of
the inscribed circle to the centre of the sector (along the angle
bisector).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $O$ be the centre of the big circle, $C$ the centre of the
inscribed circle. $C$ lies on the angle bisector at distance
$|OC| = R - r$ from $O$ (so the inscribed circle is tangent to the
arc). The perpendicular distance from $C$ to either radius of the
sector is $r$ ‚Äî so $|OC| \sin(\theta/2) = r$. Combining:
$(R - r) \sin(\theta/2) = r$, hence $r = \dfrac{R \sin(\theta/2)}
{1 + \sin(\theta/2)}$. **QED.**
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Planimetry*, ¬ß¬ß220‚Äì245.

**K1.** In $\triangle ABC$, points $D, E, F$ are taken on sides
$BC, CA, AB$ respectively, with $|BD|/|BC| = |CE|/|CA| = |AF|/|AB|
= 1/3$. Show that the area of $\triangle DEF$ is $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of
$\triangle ABC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Each of the three "corner" triangles $\triangle ADF$, $\triangle
BEF$, $\triangle CDE$ has area $\tfrac{2}{9}$ of the whole. Sum
their areas and subtract from the whole.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle ADF$: $|AF|/|AB| = 1/3$ and the foot $D$ divides $BC$ at
$2/3$ from $B$, so the cevian $AD$ has length related to the
opposite-side configuration. Each "corner" triangle has area
$\tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{2}{9}$ of the whole (using
the *side-product formula for triangle area inside a triangle*).
Three corners: $\tfrac{6}{9} = \tfrac{2}{3}$. Inner triangle:
$1 - \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{1}{3}$. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß236 ‚Äî
"*the inner triangle of one-third division*". Kiselev's elegant
result; the general case is **Routh's theorem**.)*
</details>

**K2.** A circle of radius $r$ is inscribed in a right triangle
with legs $a, b$ and hypotenuse $c$. Show that $r = \dfrac{a + b - c}{2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Use $A = rs$. Right-triangle area: $A = \tfrac{1}{2} a b$.
Semi-perimeter: $s = (a + b + c)/2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = \tfrac{1}{2} ab$ and $A = rs = r \cdot (a + b + c)/2$. So
$ab = r(a + b + c)$, i.e. $r = \dfrac{ab}{a + b + c}$. Multiply
top and bottom by $(a + b - c)$: $r = \dfrac{ab(a + b - c)}
{(a + b + c)(a + b - c)} = \dfrac{ab(a + b - c)}{(a + b)^2 - c^2}$.
By Pythagoras, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$, so
$(a + b)^2 - c^2 = 2ab$. Hence $r = \dfrac{a + b - c}{2}$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß244 ‚Äî *the right-triangle inradius
formula*.)*
</details>

**K3.** A circle is inscribed in a regular hexagon of side $a$,
and another circle is circumscribed about the hexagon. Find the
**ratio** of the two circles' areas.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
For a regular hexagon: circumradius $= a$, apothem $= a \sqrt 3 /
2$. The circles have these radii.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Inscribed circle (touching the sides) has radius $\rho = a \sqrt 3
/ 2$. Circumscribed circle (through vertices) has radius $R = a$.
Areas: $\pi \rho^2 = \tfrac{3}{4} \pi a^2$ and $\pi R^2 = \pi a^2$.
Ratio: $\dfrac{\pi \rho^2}{\pi R^2} = \tfrac{3}{4}$. **The
inscribed circle is three-fourths the area of the circumscribed.**
*(Planimetry ¬ß241 exercise.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book I ‚Äî Planimetry*, ¬ß¬ß216‚Äì245 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
K1 generalises to *Routh's theorem*; Kiselev's ¬ß236 gives only the
1/3 case but explicitly notes the generalisation.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. The [parallelogram](/glossary.qmd#parallelogram) area $b h$ comes
   from the rectangle by a *cut-and-slide* ‚Äî a shear preserves area.
2. The [triangle](/glossary.qmd#triangle) is half a parallelogram on
   the same base and height; the trapezoid is the average of its
   bases times its height ‚Äî every polygon area is built from these.
3. The circle's [area](/glossary.qmd#area) $\pi r^2$ is the limit of
   regular-$n$-gon areas ‚Äî Eudoxus' *method of exhaustion*, the
   ancestor of integral calculus.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Area scales linearly with side length."** It does not. Area
   scales by the **square** of the linear ratio. A triangle with
   doubled sides has *quadrupled* area. This is the source of the
   *square-cube law* in biology and engineering.
2. **"Heron's formula needs a right angle."** It does not ‚Äî Heron's
   formula works for *any* triangle whose three side lengths form a
   valid triangle (triangle inequality). The Pythagorean special
   case is just one application.
3. **"$\pi r^2$ is the formula for circumference."** No ‚Äî
   *circumference* is $2 \pi r$. *Area* is $\pi r^2$. The two are
   related but distinct.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive the parallelogram-area formula (Problem 1) by
   the cut-and-slide argument on a fresh sheet?
2. Did Heron's formula (Problem 4) feel like reachable algebra, or
   like memorisation? Re-do the difference-of-squares step.
3. Can you compute the area of a regular hexagon of side $s$ in
   one mental step, and the area of an equilateral triangle of
   side $s$ likewise?

- **3 yes:** **You have completed Kiselev *Planimetry* in
  Russian-classical style.** Continue to *Stereometry* (kg2) or
  return to a synthesis tier for breadth.
- **2 yes:** redo the Heron derivation ‚Äî it is the chapter's
  algebraic centrepiece.
- **0‚Äì1 yes:** Problems 1, 2, 3 form the area chain;
  re-walk them.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg1.similarity`](../07-similarity/index.qmd) ‚Äî area-scales-by-$k^2$
  used everywhere; also the triangle-area-by-altitude.
- [`kg1.regular-polygons`](../08-regular-polygons/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  the limit definition of $\pi$ via $n$-gon perimeters/areas.

### What this unlocks

- [`kg2.cylinder-cone`](../../tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/03-cylinder-cone/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  surface and volume formulas in 3D inherit the inscribed-polygon-
  limit logic from this chapter.
- [`t4.definite-integral`](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  the Riemann integral generalises the polygon-limit-equals-region
  argument used in Problem 6.
- [`t6.fourier-series`](../../tier-6-undergrad-advanced/12-fourier-series/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  *Parseval's identity* is the area-under-a-function version of the
  Pythagorean theorem in function space.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book I ‚Äî Chapter 9: Areas (¬ß¬ß216‚Äì245).** Givental
  translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)
  (the *Touch* of area as counting unit squares);
  [`cbse9.herons-formula`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/10-herons-formula/index.qmd)
  (board-exam Heron problems);
  [`cbse10.areas-related-to-circles`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/11-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd)
  (sectors and segments).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full Heron
  derivation (¬ß234) and the area-of-a-circle limit proof (¬ß241).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Polygon area is computed *every frame* for
collision response, light maps, and texture atlasing. The
*shoelace formula* (a Cartesian-coordinate version of Heron) is the
runtime workhorse. Kiselev's synthetic argument (cut-and-slide) is
the geometric *why*; the shoelace is the algorithmic *how*.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** **Image classification** loss functions are typically
computed as *integrals over the prediction landscape* ‚Äî areas in
high-dimensional space. The intuition that area is "what fits
inside" lives on; the formulas generalise to **measure theory**
([`t7.measure-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/01-measure-theory/index.qmd)).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** **The isoperimetric inequality** ‚Äî among all plane
figures of a given perimeter, the circle has the largest area ‚Äî
is the gateway to a vast theory in calculus of variations and
mathematical physics. Kiselev's chapter sets up the *area
language* in which it can be stated.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the ¬ß¬ß216‚Äì245 ordering); Heron of
  Alexandria (the formula); Eudoxus (the limit argument for the
  circle area).
- **The *cut-and-slide* proof of parallelogram area (Problem 1)** is
  the cleanest in the literature ‚Äî and it generalises to *equal
  areas under shears*, the seed of integral calculus's
  change-of-variables.
- **What this lesson does not do:** prove the *isoperimetric
  inequality*. That belongs to a separate undergraduate-level
  treatment.
- **Kiselev *Planimetry* ‚Äî track complete.** Nine chapters, all
  aligned to Givental's English translation. The natural next
  step is *Stereometry* (kg2) for the three-dimensional version,
  or *Algebra* (ka1, ka2) for the parallel non-geometry track.
:::
